hints: Write position as a sum of 16 unit vectors in random directions.
E[|ΣVᵢ|²]=ΣE[|Vᵢ|²]+cross terms; cross terms vanish by symmetry.
solution: The displacement is $\sum_{i=1}^{16}V_i$ of unit vectors in independent uniform directions. Then $\mathbb{E}[|\sum V_i|^2]=\sum\mathbb{E}[|V_i|^2]+\sum_{i\ne j}\mathbb{E}[V_i\cdot V_j]=16+0=16.$

hints: P(2 gray | top)=0.4²=0.16; P(2 gray | bottom)=0.7²=0.49.
Both shelves equally likely a priori.
P(lose game n)=(n+1)/(n+2); P(W≥k)=Π telescopes to 2/(k+1).
This harmonic series diverges ⇒ infinite ⇒ −1.
solution: Losing game $n$ has probability $\tfrac{n+1}{n+2}$, so $P(W\ge k)=\prod_{n=1}^{k-1}\tfrac{n+1}{n+2}=\tfrac{2}{k+1}$. Then $\mathbb{E}[W]=\sum_{k\ge1}\tfrac{2}{k+1}=\infty$, so the answer is $-1.$

hints: Total schedules = anagrams of 111222333444555 = 15!/(3!)⁵ = 168168000.
Subtract those with the restricted student in slot 1: a fraction 3/15 of the total.
solution: Total schedules are anagrams of $111222333444555$: $\dfrac{15!}{(3!)^5}=168168000$. The restricted student occupies slot 1 in a fraction $\tfrac{3}{15}=\tfrac15$ of these, so the count is $168168000\cdot\tfrac45=134534400.$

hints: From each inner square, track the random up-left/up-right walk to the top row, reflecting at the side edges.
Compute the corner-reaching probability from each of the 9 starting squares.
Average over the uniform starting square.
With k eggs and d drops, the max testable floors satisfy f(d,k)=f(d−1,k−1)+f(d−1,k)+1.
This equals C(d,1)+C(d,2)+C(d,3) for 3 eggs.
Evaluate at d=9.
P(perform & Philly)=0.8·0.1=0.08; P(perform & Baltimore)=0.2·0.8=0.16.
P(Philly | perform)=0.08/(0.08+0.16).
Condition on the first 9 dice summing to x.
The last die is uniform on 1–6, hitting each residue mod 6 exactly once.
So the total is uniform mod 6 ⇒ 1/6.
solution: Whatever the first nine dice sum to, the tenth die is uniform on $\{1,\dots,6\}$, taking each residue mod $6$ exactly once. Hence the total is uniform mod $6$, and the probability of divisibility is $\tfrac16.$

hints: 0.abcabc… = (100a+10b+c)/999, summed over distinct ordered (a,b,c).
Each digit position sums to 72·(0+…+9)=3240 over the 720 triples.
solution: Each repeating decimal equals $\tfrac{100a+10b+c}{999}$. Over the $10\cdot9\cdot8=720$ distinct ordered triples, each position's digits sum to $72(0+1+\cdots+9)=3240$, so the total is $\dfrac{111\cdot3240}{999}=360.$

hints: Stop when a draw exceeds threshold T; T solves the indifference V=E[max(x,V)]−0.02.
0.5V²−V+0.48=0 ⇒ V=0.8 (the threshold).
First draw is free, so payout=∫₀¹max(x,0.8)dx = 0.64+0.18 = 0.82.
solution: The continuation value $V$ (with the \$0.02 fee) satisfies $V=\int_0^1\max(x,V)\,dx-0.02$, i.e. $0.5V^2-V+0.48=0$, giving threshold $V=0.8$. Since the first draw is free, the expected payout is $\int_0^1\max(x,0.8)\,dx=0.64+0.18=0.82.$

hints: Equal-distance laps: average speed is the harmonic mean.
solution: With equal lap distances the average speed is the harmonic mean: $\dfrac{3}{\tfrac1{100}+\tfrac1{150}+\tfrac1{200}}=\dfrac{3}{13/600}=\dfrac{1800}{13}.$

hints: For no-intercept regression, t = r√(n−1)/√(1−r²) under H₀.
solution: The test statistic is $t=\dfrac{r\sqrt{n-1}}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}$. With $r=\tfrac1{10\sqrt3}$, $r^2=\tfrac1{300}$ and $\dfrac{r}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}=\dfrac1{\sqrt{299}}$, so $t=\sqrt{\tfrac{n-1}{299}}$. Requiring $t\ge1.645$ gives $n-1\ge1.645^2\cdot299\approx809.1$, so $n=811.$
